models.project: A study builder project with topic, format, and assembled items.
models.assemblyItem: An item in the project outline — a passage, character, theme, context, or question with personal annotation.
models.sourceItem: A suggested or searched material item available to add to the assembly.
models.sourceSection: A category of source material (passages, characters, contexts, questions, notes).

relationships: Project has many AssemblyItems in a user-defined order
SourceItems are suggestions based on the project topic, drawn from themes, cross-references, characters, and the user's journal
AssemblyItems link back to their source entity (passage, character, theme, etc.)

assemblyItems.title: Genesis 50:15-21
assemblyItems.preview: Joseph said to them, "Don't be afraid. Am I in the place of God? You intended to harm me, but God intended it for good..."
assemblyItems.annotation: Open with this. The brothers are terrified now that Jacob is dead — they think Joseph's forgiveness was conditional on their father being alive. His response reframes the entire narrative.

assemblyItems.title: Joseph
assemblyItems.preview: Sold by his brothers, imprisoned by his employer, forgotten by his allies — and still chose reconciliation over revenge when he held all the power.
assemblyItems.annotation: The sermon's anchor character. His story IS the forgiveness arc. Use his emotional journey as the structural backbone — betrayal, suffering, power, choice.

assemblyItems.title: Forgiveness
assemblyItems.preview: The decision to stop charging rent for the space someone occupies in your head
assemblyItems.annotation: Use the modern framing as the sermon's hook. This one-liner lands.

assemblyItems.title: Matthew 18:21-22
assemblyItems.preview: Then Peter came to Jesus and asked, "Lord, how many times shall I forgive my brother who sins against me? Up to seven times?" Jesus answered, "I tell you, not seven times, but seventy-seven times."
assemblyItems.annotation: The quantification question. Peter wants a number. Jesus gives infinity. Transition from Joseph's one-time choice to a way of life.

assemblyItems.title: Discussion question
assemblyItems.preview: Is there a difference between forgiving someone and trusting them again? Can you have one without the other?
assemblyItems.annotation: Close with this. Don't answer it — let the congregation sit with it.

items.title: Colossians 3:13
items.preview: Bear with each other and forgive one another if any of you has a grievance...

items.title: Luke 15:20-24
items.preview: But while he was still a long way off, his father saw him and was filled with compassion...

items.title: Matthew 6:14-15
items.preview: For if you forgive other people when they sin against you, your heavenly Father will also forgive you...

items.title: Father of the prodigal
items.preview: Ran to meet his son before the apology was finished — forgiveness as preemptive action

items.title: Esau
items.preview: Embraced Jacob after decades of betrayal — the reunion Genesis didn't have to give us

items.title: Honor-shame culture in ancient Near East
items.preview: Forgiveness in a culture where family honor was social currency — the cost was public, not just personal

items.title: Who have you forgiven but still don't trust?
What did forgiveness cost Joseph?
items.preview: He gave up the right to make them pay — and they knew it

items.title: Is forgiving someone who isn't sorry even possible?
Reflection on Colossians 3
items.preview: "Forgiveness isn't a feeling. It's a decision you make before the feeling catches up." — wrote this three months ago while studying Colossians.
